items.explanation: After deployment the cycle continues: you monitor live behavior and retrain and redeploy as conditions shift, so maintenance never truly ends. It is not that training runs forever or that code is constantly rewritten; it is that the lifecycle is a continuing loop, not a finish line.

lessons.title: Monitoring, Data Drift, Retraining, and Responsible Production
lessons.content:
Now the most important ongoing risk in production, the one that catches beginners by surprise. A model that was accurate the day you deployed it can **silently get worse over time, even though nobody changed the code**. Why? Because the **world changes**. This lesson is about spotting that decay and responding to it, plus the honest cautions that come with running models for real.

**Hook.** Imagine a road map printed years ago. The map itself never changed, but new roads were built and old ones closed, so it slowly became wrong. You did not break the map; reality moved out from under it. To stay useful, the map must be **updated**.

**Build.** A deployed model learns patterns from its **training data**. Over time, the **live data** it sees can drift away from that training data: new slang appears, fraudsters invent new tricks, a sensor ages and reads differently, shopping habits shift. When the live data no longer matches what the model learned, its predictions get worse. This gradual mismatch is called **data drift** (or **concept drift**, when the very meaning of the right answer changes). Crucially, this is **not** a bug in the code; it is the model going stale as the world moves. This is a real way machine learning differs from ordinary software, which does not simply rot just because the world changed, the way a model's accuracy can.

The response has two parts. First, **monitoring**: continuously watch the model's **live performance** and its **inputs**, and set **alerts** so you are warned when quality slips or the incoming data looks different from before. Second, when it degrades, **retrain** the model on **fresh, recent data** so it relearns the new patterns, then **redeploy** the updated model. Together, monitoring plus retraining is what keeps a model accurate as conditions change, closing the loop you saw earlier.

A few more production-grade practices, in plain words. **A/B testing** means trying a new model on only a **slice of traffic** and comparing it against the old one before trusting it widely. **Rollback** means switching back to the previous model if the new one turns out worse. And some honest cautions: watch for **bias or unfairness** and unexpected failures once real users arrive; keep a **human in the loop** for high-stakes decisions (like medical or legal calls) rather than letting the model decide alone; and **log the predictions** so that when something goes wrong, you can trace what happened and why.

**Recap.** Building the model is only the start. In production a model can quietly decay as live data drifts from training data, that is data or concept drift, so you monitor live performance and inputs, set alerts, and retrain on fresh data when it slips. Add A/B testing, rollback, fairness checks, a human in the loop for high-stakes calls, and logging, and you keep an ML system reliable, reproducible, and accurate as the world changes.

items.prompt: What is 'data drift' (or 'concept drift') for a deployed model?
choices: A bug that corrupts the model's code file
When the live data gradually moves away from the training data, so the model's predictions get worse over time
When the model is copied to a faster computer
When the training data is deleted by accident
items.explanation: Data drift is the live inputs slowly diverging from what the model was trained on, which degrades its predictions even though the code is unchanged. It is not a corrupted file or a hardware move; it is the world shifting away from the patterns the model learned.

items.prompt: A fraud-detection model was accurate at launch but is slowly missing more fraud each month, and no one changed its code. What is the most likely explanation?
choices: The accuracy of a model can never change once deployed
The computer is running too fast
The model finished training, so it is now permanently perfect
Data drift: fraudsters changed their tricks, so live data no longer matches the training data
items.explanation: Fraudsters inventing new tactics is a textbook cause of drift: the live data shifts away from the training data and accuracy decays. A deployed model's accuracy absolutely can change as the world moves, so assuming it stays fixed or 'permanently perfect' is exactly the misconception to avoid.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best matches data drift?
choices: A road map that goes stale as roads are built and closed, so it must be updated
A calculator that always gives the same answer to the same sum
A book that never changes its words
A clock that keeps perfect time forever
items.explanation: A map slowly becoming wrong as the world's roads change captures drift: the model is unchanged but reality moved, so it must be refreshed. The other examples stay correct on their own and do not 'go stale' as conditions change, so they miss the point of drift.

items.prompt: Together, what do MONITORING and RETRAINING accomplish?
choices: They make the model train faster the first time
They remove the need to ever deploy the model
They guarantee the model will never need any updates
They detect when a model's quality slips and refresh it on new data, keeping it accurate as conditions change
items.explanation: Monitoring spots degradation by watching live performance and inputs, and retraining relearns the new patterns from fresh data, so together they keep a deployed model accurate over time. They do not speed up first-time training or eliminate deployment; in fact they exist precisely because models do need ongoing updates.

items.prompt: What is the point of 'monitoring' a model in production?
choices: To continuously watch live performance and inputs and alert you when quality slips or the data looks different
To make the model file smaller on disk
To pick the model's name
To delete the training data automatically
items.explanation: Monitoring means keeping an eye on how the model performs on live data and whether incoming inputs have shifted, raising alerts when something degrades. It is an early-warning system for drift and failures, not a tool for shrinking files or naming or deleting things.

items.prompt: How does an ML model's behavior over time differ from ordinary software?
choices: An ML model's accuracy can quietly decay as the world changes, while ordinary software does not simply rot just because the world changed
Ordinary software and ML models both rot automatically every week
ML models never change, but ordinary software always does
There is no difference at all
items.explanation: Ordinary software keeps doing exactly what it was written to do unless someone changes it, but an ML model can lose accuracy as live data drifts from its training data. That silent, world-driven decay is the key difference, which is why monitoring and retraining matter so much for ML.

items.prompt: What does 'A/B testing' a new model mean?
choices: Training the model with both letters A and B as labels
Deleting model A and keeping model B at random
Trying the new model on only a slice of traffic and comparing it against the current one before trusting it widely
Running the model twice on the same input
items.explanation: A/B testing sends part of the live traffic to the new model and the rest to the old one, then compares results so you adopt the new model only if it actually does better. It is a careful comparison on real traffic, not a labeling scheme or a coin-flip deletion.

items.prompt: A newly deployed model turns out to perform worse than the old one. What practice lets you respond safely?
choices: Rollback: switch back to the previous, known-good model
Ignore it, since new is always better
Immediately delete all versions of the model
Stop monitoring so you do not notice the problem
items.explanation: Rollback means reverting to the previous model that you know worked, which is why keeping versioned models matters. Assuming new is always better, deleting everything, or turning off monitoring would all make the situation worse rather than safely recovering from a bad release.

items.prompt: Which is a responsible production practice when a model makes high-stakes decisions (e.g. medical or legal)?
choices: Let the model decide entirely on its own to save time
Keep a human in the loop, watch for bias or unfairness, and log predictions so problems can be traced
Turn off all logging to keep things simple
Assume the model can never be biased or fail
items.explanation: For high-stakes calls you keep a human in the loop, watch for bias and unexpected failures, and log predictions so issues can be investigated. Letting the model act alone, disabling logging, or assuming it cannot fail removes exactly the safeguards that responsible deployment depends on.

items.prompt: Why is logging a model's predictions in production useful?
choices: It makes the model train without any data
It guarantees the model is always correct
It records what the model did so that when something goes wrong, you can trace and understand it
It permanently prevents data drift from ever happening
items.explanation: Logging predictions creates a record you can look back on to trace and diagnose problems when they appear. It does not train the model, make it infallible, or stop drift; it is about traceability so issues in production can be understood and fixed.

items.prompt: Pulling it together: which statement best sums up the role of MLOps?
choices: Once a model works in a notebook, the job is finished
MLOps is only about making predictions faster
MLOps means never deploying a model, to avoid all risk
Building the model is only the start; MLOps keeps it reliable, reproducible, and accurate as the world changes
items.explanation: MLOps takes over after the model first works, handling deployment, reproducibility, monitoring, and retraining so the system stays dependable as conditions shift. Thinking the job ends at a working notebook, or that MLOps is just about speed or avoiding deployment, misses its core purpose of maintaining models in the real world.
